risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "451 eligible study participants were randomly assigned to 1 of 3 treatment groups" Comment: The article states that participants were randomly assigned, but it does not describe the actual randomization method (e.g., computer-generated sequence, random number table, blocks, or stratification). Per the review rules, 'randomly assigned' alone without additional context is insufficient to confirm a truly random sequence, so the risk remains unclear.

_evidence.method_quote: 451 eligible study participants were randomly assigned to 1 of 3 treatment groups: (1) dispersible Zn tablet, containing 5 mg Zn as zinc sulfate (Nutriset S.A.S, Malauney, France; ZnTab), (2) liquid Zn supplement, containing 5 mg Zn as zinc sulfate per 5 ml (ZnLiq), or (3) liquid placebo supplement.
_evidence.method_summary: Participants were randomly assigned to one of three treatment groups.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The article states that participants were "randomly assigned to 1 of 3 treatment groups" and that the trial was "randomized, partially-masked, placebo-controlled," but it does not describe any concealment mechanism preventing foreknowledge of the next assignment. Comment: Random assignment and masking describe trial design, not allocation concealment. Because there is no explicit mention of central allocation, SNOSE, sequentially numbered identical containers, or another mechanism that would stop the recruiter from predicting assignments, the allocation concealment risk remains unclear.

_evidence.note: The article describes random assignment and says it was a randomized, partially-masked, placebo-controlled intervention trial, but it does not describe how the allocation sequence was concealed from the recruiter.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The parent study was designed as a randomized, partially-masked, placebo-controlled intervention trial" Comment: The study explicitly reports masking and use of a placebo control, and the outcomes reported here are objective laboratory measures (e.g., plasma zinc, urinary lactulose/mannitol, plasma citrulline), so knowledge of allocation is unlikely to have influenced the dominant outcomes.

_evidence.blinding_quote: “The parent study was designed as a randomized, partially-masked, placebo-controlled intervention trial”
_evidence.blinding_method: placebo-controlled intervention trial; liquid placebo supplement; ZnTab and ZnLiq compared with placebo supplement

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Urinary lactulose and mannitol concentrations ... were measured at the Division of Biological Anthropology, University of Cambridge ... by using enzyme kinetic assays ... Urinary creatinine was measured colorimetrically ... Hemoglobin concentration ... with a HemoCue 201+ photometer ... Plasma zinc concentrations were analyzed by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrophotometry (ICP-OES) ... Plasma citrulline concentrations were performed using ... Plasma CRP and AGP concentrations were measured by a combined sandwich ELISA ... Plasma HRP2 concentrations ... were measured colorimetrically using a commercially available kit." Comment: The outcomes were assessed by laboratory/automated measurement systems rather than by clinicians or participants, and all reported outcomes are objective biochemical measures. Even though explicit statement of assessor blinding is absent, knowledge of allocation is unlikely to have influenced measurement of these lab-based outcomes; there are no subjective outcomes in the mix.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: The blood was collected in evacuated, trace element-free polyethylene tubes containing lithium heparin (Sarstedt AG & Co, Numbrecht, Germany); standard collection and processing techniques, as recommended by the International Zinc Nutrition Consultative Group (IZiNCG), were followed to prevent zinc contamination (31). Urinary lactulose and mannitol concentrations (mg/dL) were measured at the Division of Biological Anthropology, University of Cambridge (Cambridge, UK) by using enzyme kinetic assays, as described previously (34, 35), and adapted for analysis with an iEMS Reader MF (Labsystems Ltd. Helsinki, Finland). To adjust for renal clearance, urinary creatinine was measured colorimetrically using a commercially available kit ... at the USDA Western Human Nutrition Research Center (Davis, CA). Hemoglobin concentration at baseline was analyzed immediately following the capillary blood collection with a HemoCue 201+ photometer ... Plasma zinc concentrations were analyzed by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrophotometry (ICP-OES ...). Plasma citrulline concentrations were performed using ... The inter-assay CV was < 6%. Plasma CRP and AGP concentrations were measured by a combined sandwich ELISA ... Plasma HRP2 concentrations ... were measured colorimetrically ...

outcomes.label: urinary lactulose and mannitol recovery / L:M ratio

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Of 451 participants enrolled in the main zinc supplementation study, the L:M test was completed successfully in 282 (63%) Predominant reasons for unsuccessful tests were incomplete consumption of test sugars (N = 70, 16%) and leakage or fecal contamination of urine samples (N = 67, 15%)" Comment: For the analysed outcome (L:M test), only 282/451 participants contributed data, implying about 37% missing outcome data. The missingness was substantial and no per-arm analysed counts or missing-data sensitivity analysis are reported to show robustness, so this meets the high-risk threshold for attrition bias.

per_arm_flow.reasons: 451 eligible study participants were randomly assigned to 1 of 3 treatment groups: (1) dispersible Zn tablet, containing 5 mg Zn as zinc sulfate (ZnTab)
451 eligible study participants were randomly assigned to 1 of 3 treatment groups: (2) liquid Zn supplement, containing 5 mg Zn as zinc sulfate per 5 ml (ZnLiq)
451 eligible study participants were randomly assigned to 1 of 3 treatment groups: (3) liquid placebo supplement

_evidence.notes: Analysed outcome reported for the L:M test subset: 'Of 451 participants enrolled ... the L:M test was completed successfully in 282 (63%)'. The analysed sample was reduced further for some measures (e.g., N=279 for L:C and M:C ratios; N=80 for plasma citrulline), but no per-arm analysed counts were provided.
